expectations.text: (e1-rebase-first) Response recommends rebasing on origin/main BEFORE pushing
expectations.evidence: The response goes directly from committing (Step 2) to pushing (Step 3) with no mention of fetching or rebasing on origin/main. There is no `git fetch origin`, `git rebase origin/main`, or equivalent step anywhere in the response.

expectations.text: (e1-force-with-lease) Response mentions `--force-with-lease` for pushing after a rebase
expectations.evidence: The literal text '--force-with-lease' does not appear anywhere in the response. The only push commands shown are `git push -u origin feat/payments` and `git push`, with no mention of force-with-lease.

expectations.text: (e1-cleanup-sequence) Response includes post-merge cleanup: worktree remove + branch delete + prune
expectations.evidence: The cleanup section (line 68-74) includes `git worktree remove <path-to-worktree>` and `git branch -d feat/payments`, but omits `git worktree prune`. All three steps (worktree remove, branch delete, AND prune) are required for this assertion to pass.

expectations.text: (e1-conventional-title) Response uses conventional commits format for the PR title (type(scope): subject)
expectations.evidence: The response demonstrates conventional commits format in two places: the commit message example `"feat(payments): your commit message"` (line 22) and the PR title example `"feat(payments): <short description>"` (line 44), both following the type(scope): subject pattern.

suggestions: Add a 'sync with main' step before pushing: run `git fetch origin` followed by `git rebase origin/main` (or `git merge origin/main`) to ensure the branch is up to date before opening a PR.
After recommending a rebase, note that if the branch was already pushed, a `git push --force-with-lease` is needed to update the remote — explaining why --force-with-lease is safer than --force.
Complete the post-merge cleanup sequence by adding `git worktree prune` after `git worktree remove` and `git branch -d` to remove stale worktree tracking references.
eval_feedback.overall: The response covers the basic push-and-PR workflow and correctly uses conventional commits format for example titles. However, it misses three important best practices for worktree-based development: syncing with the upstream main branch before pushing (rebase-first), safe force-pushing after a rebase (--force-with-lease), and the complete post-merge cleanup sequence (missing git worktree prune). These gaps could lead to messy PR diffs, diverged history, or stale worktree entries in the repository.
